title: Quick check: the portal's global view

questions.prompt: Where should you start your shift in the portal?

choices.label: Organisations — click through each customer to see what's changed.
choices.rationale: With many orgs this takes an hour and most won't have moved. Wrong starting surface.

choices: Incidents — filtered to open and Critical-first, then walk down by severity.

choices.label: Reports — pull last week's summary and read it.
choices.rationale: Reports are for monthly client comms, not shift catch-up.

choices.label: Settings — confirm nothing has changed in the platform overnight.
choices.rationale: Settings doesn't normally change between shifts.

questions.explanation: The Incidents list sorts by attention required; Critical-first means you don't miss something that's been waiting.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a top-level item the lesson lists in the portal's global view?

choices.label: Organisations
choices.rationale: It is a top-level item — the per-customer list.

choices.label: Identity
choices.rationale: It is a top-level item — the ITDR roll-up.

choices.label: Incidents
choices.rationale: It is a top-level item, and one of the most-used ones.

questions.explanation: There's no top-level Quarantine surface in Huntress — that's an AV-product concept that doesn't map here.

questions.prompt: A peer says: "I filtered Incidents by Critical and the page is full of old closed incidents — the filter is broken." What's the likely real issue?

choices.label: Their permission set is wrong; they need a senior to fix their account.
choices.rationale: Permissions wouldn't surface only old closed Criticals; they'd show no Criticals at all.

choices: They've filtered by severity but forgotten to filter by status;

choices.label: The portal is having an outage; check the status page.
choices.rationale: Possible but unlikely as the first guess. Severity-without-status is the named common mistake.

choices.label: Browser cache is stale — clear cookies.
choices.rationale: The filter behaviour is by-design, not a caching issue.

questions.explanation: Critical without status: open pulls in months of closed Criticals. Severity AND status together. Critical without open pulls in months of closed Criticals.

questions.prompt: A new tech asks: "Why don't I see a SIEM item in the global view? My peer's portal has one." What's the most likely explanation?
choices: The peer's MSP subscribes to Managed SIEM and yours doesn't;

choices.label: The peer is on a beta version of the portal.
choices.rationale: The visibility is subscription-driven, not version-driven.

choices.label: The new tech needs a senior to enable SIEM on their account.
choices.rationale: It's not an account-level toggle for the tech to enable; it's the partner's subscription.

choices.label: SIEM is being deprecated and is hidden by default.
choices.rationale: Wrong on the facts. SIEM launched April 2025 and is active.

questions.explanation: SIEM only appears when the partner subscribes. SIEM is visible only when the partner subscribes.

questions.prompt: You log in at the start of a shift after three days off. You need to catch up. Where do you go first?

choices.label: Organisations — open each customer and check what's happened.
choices.rationale: With many orgs this takes an hour. Most haven't moved. Start where the change is.

choices: Incidents — filter to open and Critical-first, then walk down by severity.

choices.label: Reports — pull last week's summary and read it cover to cover.
choices.rationale: Reports aggregate for monthly client comms; not the right granularity for catch-up.

choices.label: Agents — confirm every endpoint is online.
choices.rationale: Agent-health auditing isn't the catch-up surface.

questions.explanation: The Incidents view is the catch-up surface; Critical-first means you don't miss something that's been waiting.

questions.prompt: You filter Incidents to "open, Critical-first" and find three Critical incidents in the list, all tagged with the same organisation. What does the pattern tell you, and what do you do?

choices.label: One customer is having a bad day. Open the first incident and work it; the rest can wait.
choices.rationale: Half right. Three Criticals in one org is itself a signal worth flagging.

choices: Open the first incident, escalate the cluster to a senior in parallel, and keep working.

choices.label: Switch into that organisation and treat the three incidents as one investigation.
choices.rationale: Critical incidents are contained one at a time per the playbook; bundling them is senior territory.

choices.label: Wait for the senior to contact you about the cluster.
choices.rationale: Reactive; the lesson teaches proactive escalation when you see the pattern.

questions.explanation: Three Criticals on one tenant is tell-the-senior-in-parallel territory; contain and flag.

questions.prompt: You need to find a specific agent on a host called WS-RECEPTION-02 and you're not sure which customer it belongs to. Where's the right place to look?
choices: The global Agents view; filter or search across organisations to locate the host.

choices.label: Open each customer's Organisation page and look at their Agents sub-view.
choices.rationale: Slow and wrong-shaped. Global Agents is the cross-org tool.

choices.label: Search the Reports area for a hostname mention.
choices.rationale: Reports aggregate; not the right surface for one-off agent lookup.

choices.label: Contact Huntress support and ask them to look up the host.
choices.rationale: Misuses support. The portal answers this directly.

questions.explanation: Cross-org agent lookup is exactly what the global Agents view is for.

questions.prompt: This lesson is navigation-only, so the escalation surface is narrow. Which of these DOES warrant flagging to a senior?

choices.label: You navigated to the wrong page and corrected yourself.
choices.rationale: The lesson is explicit: that's a quiet self-correction.

choices: You can't see something you expect to see.

choices.label: A particular filter combo doesn't return the results you expect.
choices.rationale: Filter-tuning is solvable at the tech level.

choices.label: An incident is taking longer than you'd like to load.
choices.rationale: Performance bumps don't need senior involvement.

questions.explanation: (an organisation, a surface, a SIEM section you know should be there);. A permissions or licensing question. Missing expected access is the legitimate escalation surface — don't work around it.

questions.prompt: The lesson says you don't need to memorise the portal — Huntress changes it every few months. What does the lesson say you DO need to do?

choices.label: Re-read the navigation lesson every quarter to keep current.
choices.rationale: Not the lesson's recommendation.

choices: Know where each thing roughly lives so you don't waste minutes finding the right menu item.

choices.label: Bookmark every page you visit so you can navigate by URL.
choices.rationale: Bookmarks rot when the portal renames pages.

choices.label: Memorise the URL pattern for each surface so you can deep-link from the PSA.
choices.rationale: URL patterns shift with portal changes too.

questions.explanation: Orientation, not deep dive — speed comes from knowing roughly where to look.
